planting.note: Plant bulbs just below the soil surface (neck at or slightly below soil level) in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after frost; blood lily blooms most reliably in partial shade with consistent moisture and warmth — drought stress causes the bulb to go dormant prematurely without blooming.

directions: Plant bulbs just below soil surface in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; blood lily (fireball lily, powder puff lily) is an African tropical bulb that produces one of the most spectacular and unexpected flower displays of any shade-tolerant bulb — a single large, smooth, spherical, perfectly round, 4–6-inch-diameter ball composed entirely of several hundred individual small, bright scarlet to orange-red, star-shaped flowers with prominent, projecting, golden-tipped stamens, rising on a stout, purple-mottled stem directly from the soil before or simultaneously with the emerging leaf growth. The ball-like flower head has a precise, geometric, almost artificial perfection that makes it appear more like a designed object than a natural flower. It blooms in spring to early summer, then produces a large rosette of broad, soft, tropical-looking leaves that persist through the growing season before dying back in winter. Unlike the related Haemanthus, Scadoxus multiflorus does not tolerate complete drought dormancy and performs best with some moisture year-round. A dramatic and relatively easily grown shade garden bulb for zone 9–12 tropical and subtropical gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or slightly above the soil surface (unlike most bulbs) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to sandy soil in partial to full shade; white paintbrush lily is evergreen and should not be allowed to dry out completely — unlike most amaryllid bulbs, it does not require a dry dormant period and performs best with consistent, moderate moisture year-round.

directions: Plant bulbs with neck at or above soil level in well-drained, average soil in partial to full shade; white paintbrush lily is a South African evergreen bulb of the amaryllis family that is one of the easiest and most adaptable shade bulbs available for zone 9–12 gardens — a low-growing, tough, long-lived, evergreen plant producing a pair of broad, strap-shaped, dark-green, leathery, persistently glossy leaves that lie nearly flat on the ground and persist year-round, providing attractive foliage groundcover even out of flower. In autumn and winter, short stems bear compact, white, paintbrush-like flower heads — dense spherical clusters of small, white, tubular flowers with prominent white stamens creating a soft, fluffy, white-brush effect — followed by orange-red berries that are ornamental through winter. The primary virtue of white paintbrush lily is its exceptional shade and drought tolerance among evergreen bulbs; it grows remarkably well in the dry, deep shade under mature trees where most other bulbs and plants fail. It clumps and multiplies slowly by offsets. An underused but reliable and handsome shade groundcover bulb for zones 9–12.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; agapanthus blooms most freely when root-bound and when the bulb-like roots can see some light — avoid deep planting and unnecessary disturbance, as established clumps bloom most reliably when left undivided.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade; African lily is a South African evergreen perennial that has become one of the most widely planted flowering perennials in warm-climate gardens worldwide — producing large, round, elegant, spherical flower heads of 20–40 individual, tubular, funnel-shaped, bright blue to violet-blue or white flowers on strong, upright, 2–4-foot stems above broad rosettes of glossy, strap-shaped, dark-green, evergreen leaves, blooming in summer (June–August). The spherical blue flower heads are among the most refined and beautifully structured of any summer-blooming perennial and associate superbly with white and yellow flowers. Agapanthus praecox (syn. A. orientalis) is the largest and most common species in cultivation, forming substantial clumps that flower most freely when moderately crowded and root-bound; excessive division reduces flowering. Tolerates partial shade, clay soil, coastal salt spray, and moderate drought once established. The deciduous species (A. campanulatus, A. caulescens) are hardier (to zone 6) but less evergreen; A. praecox is evergreen but limited to zone 8–12.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring after frost; yellow calla lily requires dry summer dormancy (reduce or stop watering after the leaves die back) to initiate the following season's flower buds — gardeners who keep the soil moist through summer typically get good foliage but poor flower production the following season.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; yellow calla lily is a South African summer-dormant bulb that produces large, elegant, golden-yellow calla spathe flowers similar in form to the familiar white calla (Zantedeschia aethiopica) but in a warm, deep-yellow to butter-yellow color that is distinctly different in character — the yellow spathe surrounding the upright, golden-yellow spadix is held on a stout stem above large, semi-upright, heart-shaped, dark-green leaves that are ornamentally decorated with translucent white spots and blotches (a characteristic feature of this species). The spotted foliage is itself ornamental throughout the growing season. Unlike the common white calla, yellow calla lily requires a dry summer dormancy for good subsequent blooming — reducing water after leaf dieback allows the tuber to ripen and develop its flower buds. Hardy to approximately 15°F in zone 8 with good drainage. The yellow and orange-flowered spotted-leaf Zantedeschia species (Z. elliottiana, Z. pentlandii, Z. rehmannii) are the parents of the modern garden calla hybrid cultivars in yellow, orange, red, pink, and purple.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide corms or separate offsets in spring, or plant container-grown specimens in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial to full shade after frost; silver cloud taro is more shade-tolerant than most colocasias and performs better in deep shade with consistent moisture than in full sun, where the silvery leaf markings may bleach.

directions: Divide corms or plant container specimens in moist to wet, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial to full shade; silver cloud taro is a smaller, more refined, and more shade-tolerant Colocasia species from Southeast Asia — unlike the bold, large-scale giant taros (C. esculenta, C. gigantea), this species produces compact, 18–30-inch plants with relatively smaller but more ornately marked leaves, each heart-shaped, dark-green leaf decorated with a distinctive, broad, silvery-gray to silver-white zone in the center of the leaf surface that fades to dark-green at the margins — creating a soft, clouded, misty silvery-green effect that justifies its cultivar name 'Silver Cloud'. The silvery leaf marking is unique among colocasias and creates a quieter, more refined tropical foliage effect than the large, plain green forms. It grows best in consistently moist to wet soil in partial to full shade, where the silvery marking is most pronounced. More cold-tolerant than most colocasias (to approximately 20°F in zone 8 with protection). Excellent in shaded water gardens, bog gardens, and as a refined shade groundcover under large trees in zone 8–12.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
